risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated randomization table was created by the research pharmacist and used to randomize participants." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence prepared by the pharmacy, which is a proper random method and sufficient for Low risk.

Quote: "Participants underwent a comprehensive medical evaluation, baseline assessments and were randomized by the pharmacy in a one-to-one fashion." / "A computer-generated randomization table was created by the research pharmacist and used to randomize participants." / "Placebo was normal saline in identical bottles and labels." Comment: The paper explicitly states that randomization was performed by the pharmacy/research pharmacist, which is consistent with a pharmacy-controlled allocation process. This is an explicit mechanism that would prevent the enrolling clinician from foreseeing the next assignment, so allocation concealment is judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel design trial of IN-OXT versus placebo in adults with ASD." Comment: The study explicitly reports double blinding and used placebo in identical bottles/labels, which indicates participants and personnel delivering treatment were intended to be blinded. The outcomes assessed (e.g. repetitive behaviors, social cognition, quality of life, CGI) could in principle be influenced by knowledge of allocation, but the reported methods support successful blinding and do not suggest it was broken.

Quote: "All efficacy assessments were carried out by an independent evaluator who was blinded to both side effects and group assignment. All safety evaluations were performed by a physician who was blinded to group assignment. Although we are not aware of any characteristic side effects of IN-OXT, this approach assured that all efficacy assessments were done by a truly blinded clinician." Comment: The outcome assessors were explicitly blinded. The main efficacy outcomes included clinician-rated CGI and other assessments performed by an independent blinded evaluator, and safety was assessed by a blinded physician. These outcomes are not purely patient self-report, and for the clinician-rated outcomes knowledge of allocation could have biased measurement if unblinded; however, blinding was clearly reported, so detection bias is low.

Quote: "Sixteen of the 19 patients (84%) had data at week 6. For the remaining three subjects, we imputed week 6 ratings using expectation-maximization (EM) methods and the earlier CGI ratings." Comment: Attrition was small overall (3/19, 16%) and the reasons for discontinuation were described elsewhere as due to adverse events/tolerability (staring spells, worsening tics, panic attack with nasal spray) rather than an imbalance suggesting differential outcome-related dropout. The analysis also used full-information maximum-likelihood mixed-effects models and imputation for the remaining CGI data, which is an appropriate method for handling the small amount of missing data. Overall, missing outcome data are unlikely to have biased the results.
